text: EISENACH, a principality, circle, and town of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach. The principality comprises an area of 164 sq. m., and is situated in the W part of the duchy, between the Thuringer-wald and the Rhöngebirge. It is watered by the Werra, and its affluents the Felde, Horsel, and Nesse, and is to a great extent covered with wooded mountains. This principality was added to the duchy of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach in 1741. It was previously an independent sovereignty.—The town is situated at the confluence of the Horsel with the Nesse, at the foot of the Wartsburg, at an alt. of 712 ft. above sealevel, 19 m. W of Gotha, and 45 m. W of Weimar, and on the railroad from Cassel to Leipzig. Pop. in 1834, 9,106; in 1846, 9,439. It is enclosed by a wall, and is well-built. The streets are broad and generally paved with basalt, and the market-place is handsome and commodious. The principal public buildings are the castle, formerly the residence of the princes of Eisenach, and the parish-church and town-house; but it has several other churches, a bank, two hospitals, an orphan's asylum, a house-ofcorrection, a prison, a gymnasium, and a school of design. Varieties of woollen and cotton fabrics, fine carpets, pipe-heads of soap-stone-a species of magnesian limestone-and pipes fashioned from the knotted roots of the elder-tree, are manufactured here; and there are also extensive wool and cotton spinning-mills, oil, grain, powder and paper mills, and several tanneries. The trade, which is very active, consists chiefly in grain, wine, beer, and articles of local manufacture. Fairs are held three times a-year. On a height above E. are the ruins of the ancient castle of Wartsburg, formerly the residence of the landgraves of Thuringia, and the asylum of Luther during the period of his seclusion under the arrest of the Elector of Saxony. Within its walls he executed his translation of the Bible. EISENARZ, EISENARZT, or INNERBERG, a town of Austria, in Upper Styria, in the district of Gratz, circle and 19 m. NW of Bruck, on an affluent of the Salza, and to the NW of the Eisenberg or Erzberg. It lies in a valley, but has an alt. of 2,190 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,500. It has a mining establishment, and 3 blast-furnaces, in connection with the extensive iron-mines and works of the Eisenberg, which is almost a solid block of carbonate of iron, 6,000 fath. in circumf., and 3,000 ft. in alt.
EISENBACH, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Bodensee, SE of Urach. Pop. 652.
EISENBACH, or VIHNYE, a small village of Hungary, in the comitat of Bars, 9 m. NW of Schemnitz. It is noted for its mineral waters.

EISENBERG, a circle, bailiwick, and walled town of Saxe-Altenburg, 23 m. W of Altenburg, and 29 m. E of Weimar. Pop. 4,744. It is situated on a rising ground; and has a ducal castle, an observatory, a classical school, an asylum for the poor, &c. Porcelain, cloth, and other woollen fabrics, leather, and carriages, are extensively manufactured here. The trade consists chiefly in timber. Pop. of bail. 13,000.—Also a town of Saxony, in the circle and 9 m. NNW of Dresden, near the fortress of Moritzburg. Pop. in 1834, 760. It has extensive cattlemarkets.—Also a village of Bavaria, in the circle of the Pfalz, district and NW of Durkheim. Pop. 823. It has several iron-forges, and a paper-mill.—Also a village of Electoral Hesse, in the circle and 9 m. SE of Cassel. It has an alum-mine.—Also a village of Nassau, N of Selters.
EISENBERG, or RUDA, a village of Moravia, in the circle and 28 m. WNW of Olmutz, on the March. Pop. 818. A castle, belonging to the princes of Lobkowitz, is situated in its environs.
EISENBROD, or Zelezny-Brod, a small town of Bohemia, in the circle of Bunzlau, and 24 m. NE of Jung-Bunzlau, on the r. bank of the Iser. Pop. 1,735.

EISENBURG, a comitat or administrative province and town of Hungary.—The comitat, called in Hungary Vas-Varmegye, is bounded on the N by the comitat of Oedenburg; on the E by that of Veszprim; on the SE by the comitat of Szala; and on the SW and W by Styria. It is 86 m. in length from NE to SW, and 47 m. in breadth; comprising an in Europe, in the gov. of the Don Cossacks, at the * EIS area of 96.9 German sq. m., and containing 42 towns and 612 villages. Pop. in 1837, 279,928, consisting of Hungarians, Germans, and Croats, of whom 220,000 were Catholics, 59,500 Protestants, and 30,000 Jews. It presents a generally undulating surface, and is penetrated in the W and S by several branches of the Styrian Alps. Its principal rivers are the Raab, and its tributaries the Pinka, Sorok, Herpenyo, and Giöngyos. The soil is fertile, and well-cultivated, producing grain, wine, fruit, lint, and tobacco, in large quantities. Of its arable land, 408,226 jochs are under culture; 36,940 are appropriated to the culture of the vine; and 275,626 are covered with wood. Game and bees abound in the woods, and cattle are pastured in great numbers on the mountains. It has mines of iron, mercury, manganese, sulphur, vitriol, and alum, quarries of marble, and several mineral springs. Its cap. is Güns.—The town of E., called in Hungary Vas or Vasvar, is 30 m. SSE of Güns, and 11 m. E of Kormond, near the r. bank of the Raab. Pop. 1,200. It has a Catholic church, and a convent, and was formerly fortified. The environs are noted for their wine.
EISENMARKT. See VAJDA HUNYAD.
EISENSCHMITT, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, regency and 23 m. NNE of Treves. Pop. 940. It has an extensive iron-work.

EISENSTADT, or Kis-Ma'rton, a free royal town of Hungary, in the comitat and 12 m. NNW of Oedenburg, at the foot of the Leithagebirge, and 3 m. W of the Neusiedler lake. Pop. in 1840, 5,700, of whom 826 were Jews. It is enclosed by a wall, and contains 3 principal streets, a Catholic church, 2 convents—one of which forms the place of sepulture of the princes of Esterhazy—the town-house, and an hospital. Adjacent to the town is the seignorial demesne and palace of the princes of Esterhazy. The latter, erected in 1805, is one of the richest and most magnificent buildings in the kingdom. It is in a florid Italian style; and is surrounded by gardens laid out in the English style, and orangeries and hothouses containing 70,000 exotic plants. This town suffered extensively from a fire in 1768.
EISENSTEIN, or EISENSTEIN (MARKT), a town of Bohemia, 45 m. S of Pilsen, on the confines of Bavaria, on the Regen. Pop. 457.

EISERNES-THOR, or DEMIR-KAPU, a narrow gorge, running between a ramification of the Balkan and the Zmenik, on the confines of Hungary and Turkey in Europe, through which the Danube passes in its entrance into the latter country.
EISEY, a parish of Wilts, 1 m. NNE of Cricklade, on the Thames. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 188.

EISFELD, a bailiwick and market-town of Saxe- Meiningen, in the principality and 9 m. E of Hildburghausen, and 25 m. ESE of Meiningen, in a valley, on the Werra, by which it is divided into two parts, distinguished as the old and new. Pop. 2,796. It is enclosed by a wall, and contains a castle, 3 churches, and an hospital. Cotton fabrics, leather, articles in papier mache, and nails, are manufactured here. Its trade consists chiefly in wood and leather. The bail. contains 9,180 inhabitants.

EISGRUB, or LEDNICE, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 33 m. SSE of Brünn, on the r. bank of the Thaya, on the confines of the archduchy of Austria. Pop. 1,954, of whom, in 1834, 211 were Jews. It has a magnificent castle and park, belonging to the princes of Lichtenstein.
EISHAUSEN, a village of Saxe-Meiningen, in the principality and S of Hildburghausen.
EISINGEN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, N of Pforzheim. Pop. 700.
EISKOI-OUKRIEPLENIE, a fortress of Russia entrance of the Kougoï-Jëia into a gulf of the sea of | Azov of the same name.

EISLEBEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, cap. of the circle of Mannsfield, in the regency and 27 m. NW of Merseburg, on the Böse, at an alt. of 295 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1846, 8,340. It is divided into two parts, distinguished as the new and old town, and has several suburbs. The old town is surrounded by walls and ditches. With the exception of the churches, 4 in number, an old castle, a gymnasium, and 2 hospitals, it has no buildings worthy of note. It is chiefly celebrated as the birth-place of Martin Luther. The site of the house in which he was born, and which was destroyed by fire in 1689, is now occupied by a charity school. The working of the gold and copper-mines of Mannsfeld, and the manufactory of linen, tobacco, and potash, form the chief branches of local industry.
EISLINGEN (GROSS), a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, bail. and 2 m. E of Goppingen. Pop. in 1840, 1,205.
EISNERN, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Carniola, in the circle and 28 m. WNW of Laybach. It has numerous forges and nail-manufactories.
EISTRUP, a town of Denmark, in Jutland, in the div. of Aarhuus, 25 m. WNW of Horsens.
EITERFELD, a bailiwick and town of Hesse- Cassel, in the prov. of Fulda, circle and 8 m. N of Hünfeld. Pop. 632.
EIXO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 27 m. SSW of Lamego, near the r. bank of the Vouga. Pop. 3,102.
EJDA, a village of Norway, in the bail. of Drontheim, 6 m. NW of Hov. Copper is mined in the neighbourhood, and smelted here.
EJOW, or RAJAH BASSA PEAK, a mountain on the E side of Lampun bay, in the island of Sumatra, in about 5° 49' S lat. Alt. 1,600 ft.
EKATERINBURG. See YEKATERINBURG.
EKEROE, an island in the gulf of Bothnia, in the group of the Aland isles, to the W of and separated by a narrow channel from the island of Aland. It is about 15 m. in length, and 5 m. in breadth.
EKESJO, an ancient town of Sweden, in the prefecture and 33 m. ESE of Jonkoping, at an alt. of 652 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1833, 1,497.
EKFRID, a township of Upper Canada, in the London district, bordered on the SE by the Thames. Pop. in 1842, 1,174.

EKHE, or IGA, a river of Mongolia, which issues from Lake Kussugoul; runs SE; and after receiving numerous affluents, unites with the Selenga, 120 m. WSW of the confluence of that river with the Orkhon.
EKHMIN. See AKHMIN.

EKIM-KHAN, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. and 160 m. E of Sivas, sanj. and 70 m. E of Divriki, on a rising ground near the E bank of the Euphrates. It consists of about 250 houses, wellbuilt of brick, and contains a good caravanserai, and a small mosque.
EKIZEH PASS, a defile of the Muz or Mussur- Dagh, on the NW confines of Little Bokhara, near the source of one of the head-streams of the Kashgar, and about 100 m. NW of the town of that name.
EKIZTCHE, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Bulgaria, in the sanj. and 17 m. N of Shumla.

EKNAS, or EKENAS, a town and small port of Russia in Europe, in the grand duchy of Finland, laen and 60 m. W of Helsingfors, pleasantly situated on a fiord of the gulf of Finland. Pop. 1,800. It has manufactories of woollen and linen fabrics, and of sail-cloth.
EKOSTRAVSKAIA, a town of Russian Lapland, ELB 182 on the SE bank of Lake Imandra, 300 m. NW of Archangel.
EKSHARAD, a town of Sweden, in the prefecture and 65 m. N of Carlstadt, on the r. bank of the Klar.

ELALA, or HILELA, a district of Marocco, in the prov. of Sous-al-aksa, at the foot of the Atlas mountains. Mines of silver, copper, and iron, are wrought in the vicinity. The pop. is estimated at 25,000.
ELALIA, or AL-ALIAH, a town of Tunis, 120 m. SSE of Tunis, on the E coast, to the N of Ras Kapondiah. In the vicinity are extensive ruins, supposed to be those of the ancient Acola or Acilia.
ELAMAN, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. of Aljezira, sanj. of Diar-Modzar, on the l. bank of the Euphrates, 4 m. SW of Rakka.
ELAMIRA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. of Damascus, near the S bank of the Euphrates, 5 m. ESE of Rakka.

ELANDS BERG, a mountain of South Africa, in the district of Graaf Reynet, extending along the W side of Zekoe or Sea-Cow river.—Also a mountain in the district of Uitenhage, extending from W to E between Riet-Berg and Hartebeeste.
ELANDS FOUNTAIN, a village of South Africa, in the district and 75 m. NNW of Graaf Reynet.
ELANDS VALLEY, a village of S. Africa, in the Griquas district, at the head of Hartebeeste river, and on the road from the Cape district to Griqua-town.

ELANSKAIA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the country of the Don Cossacks, on the N bank of the Don, 10 m. above the confluence of the Vorona. ELANSKOE' a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 100 m. SE of Voronej, on an affluent of the Vorona.
ELANUGGE, or LLANUGGE, a town of Africa, in the Somali territory, district and 30 m. W of Hurrur, and on the road from that town to Ancobar.
ELAPHONISI. See CERVI.
ELASERE, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Yémen, 30 m. NE of Chamir.
ELASON-NOR, a lake of Mongolia, in the district of Thsian Hai, to the SE of the Koko-Nor or Blue lake, which receives the Polonkir on the E.
ELATEA, a mountain of Greece, in Livadia, which separates the district of Attica on the NW from that of Bœotia. It is the Citheron of the ancients.
ELATO, a summit of the chain of the Pindus, in Greece, in the prov. of Livadia, 5 m. N of Salona, extending between the mountains of Vardisio and Lyakouro, or Parnassus.

ELBA, an island in the Mediterranean, the Ilva of the Etruscans and Romans, and Ætalia of the Greeks. It lies between the parallels of 42° 43' and 42° 53′ N, 30 m. E of Corsica, off the Tuscan coast, from which it is separated by the channel of Piombino, which is about 6 m. broad in its narrowest part. Cape Vita, its N extremity, bears from the town of Piombino, on the mainland, WSW & W 6 m. Its form is irregular. In circumf. it may measure nearly 60 m. Its length is 18 m.; but it scarcely in any part exceeds 10 m. in width. The coast forms a beautiful and varied succession of deep bays, guarded by rugged headlands. The superficial area is 154 sq. m. It is mountainous in its general aspect, and its plains and valleys are of small extent; but it is favoured with a salubrious climate, a good soil, and numerous springs of excellent water; and its little plains are all divided and cultivated like orchards. In marshy situations, however, malaria is said to prevail from June till October. The plain of Mola, between Longone and Capoliveri, although the most fertile soil in the island, is abandoned to stagnant waters, and consequently to malaria. The prevailing rocks are granite, clay slate, and limestone passing into marble. Its highest mountain, Monte della Capanna, at the E end of the island, has an alt. of 3,336 ft. In the vicinity of Capoliveri is the Monte Calamita, or mountain of loadstone.—Its vegetable produce consists of grass, corn, vines, olives, chestnuts, almonds, walnuts, figs, evergreen oaks, bay-laurels, myrtles, lavender, and a variety of aromatic and evergreen plants; pears, apples, cherries, peaches, and prunes are produced, but they are wild and insipid. Lemons, pomegranates, water-melons, and oranges, are also raised, though not in great perfection; and the opuntia or prickly pear, the date-palm, and the aloe, are grown. From the practice of burning for pasture, there is a want of forest-trees or large wood, except where the suggera or cork-tree abounds.—The husbandry of the island is conducted in an unskilful and slovenly manner; and the corn raised does not supply the inhabitants with more than two months' consumption. The chestnut crop affords in some measure a substitute for corn. Its produce is dried and ground into flour, from which excellent pollenta is made. The vintage, however, is more than sufficient for the wants of the pop., the average annual produce being about 185,725 barrels of 10 gallons each. The grapes are of excellent quality, and two kinds of wine are made from them,-red and white. The white is the common wine consumed in the island, and is seldom or never exported; the red is made in small quantity, but is extremely delicious. Two sorts of dessert wine are produced, viz., Vermont and Aleatico, both of which are highly esteemed. On an average of 15 years, from 1823 to 1838, the annual value of the wine and agricultural produce of the island was 4,650,214 francs. In E., as in other parts of Italy, the use of the press is unknown in the manufacture of wine. The lower grounds abound with large fig-trees.—E. has long been celebrated for its iron. Virgil describes it as "insula inexhaustis chalybum generosa metallis." There are still several mines of this metal throughout the island; but the principal, and the only one now worked, is that of Rio, near the v. of Marina, on the E coast. It consists of an entire mountain, about 2 m. in circumf., and 500 ft. in height, of which the rich magnetic ore yields from 35 to 63 per cent. of metal, and is so abundant as to afford a supply to Corsica, Genoa, Naples, and Rome, as well as Tuscany. About 1,600 centi of the ore are produced annually, each cento consisting of <math>33,333</math> Tuscan lbs. <math>= 25,000</math> English lbs. In exporting it to the neighbouring coasts, about 120 vessels of from 5 to 50 tons are employed. The price varies according to the quality of the ore, but generally runs from 50 to 52 scudi per quintal. Mines of copper also exist in E., but these are not open. There are quarries of loadstone, granite, and marble; we also meet with alabaster, steatite, asbestos, serpentine, quartz, syenite, and other minerals. The number of miners in the island in 1836 was 208.—There are few oxen or cows kept on this island; asses, mules, and horses, and a considerable number of sheep, goats, and pigs, are reared; but the breed of all these animals is small. Hares, red-legged partridges, quails, wood-pigeons, and rabbits, are in abundance. Noxious reptiles, such as the smaller scorpion, the blind worm, the adder, asp, and vipers, are said to abound; there also occurs here the spotted spider, whose bite is said to be mortal. There are two tunny-fisheries on the coast of E.: one of these is carried on at Porto- Ferrajo, the other at Marciana. The months of June and July form the fishing-season; the annual produce of both is estimated at £2,500 sterling. The bearded mullet, and that brilliant fish called by the Italians donzellina, are found in the surrounding sea. The coast of E. formerly abounded in oysters, some of It is surrounded by a wall, and contains an old castle, ELB them containing pearls of considerable size and fine colour. There are salt-marshes in the vicinity of Porto-Ferrajo and Longone, from which about 30,000 sacks of salt, containing about 200 lbs. each, are produced annually. There is no machinery on the island except that of corn-mills, and these ill-constructed and unskilfully managed. - The importations consist of grain, cheese, cattle, and other necessary articles; the exportations, of iron ore, granite, vinegar, wines, tunny, and salt. The principal places are Porto-Ferrajo, containing about 3,000 inhabitants; Rio, with about 2,000; Porto-Longone, with 1,500; Marciana, with 1,200; Campo, and Capo-Liveri. The small island of Pianosa, 95 m. S from the SW point of E., is entirely uninhabited.—In 1808, the whole pop. of the island was estimated at 12,000; in 1842 it amounted to 17,770. In 1839, out of a pop. of 17,410, 2,497 were returned as landed proprietors and members of liberal professions; 2,738 as agriculturists; 814 as artisans; and 1,436 as paupers. The character of the natives is superior to that of the Italians in general. Every man is born a seaman, and the inability to row or navigate a boat is held disgraceful. Their language is a patois, of which the radical words are Tuscan. The criminal jurisdiction of the island is appointed by the grand-duke, who bears the expense. The civil magistracy are annually chosen by lot. The gonfaloniere, or first magistrate, is taken from that class who are rated in the public valuation roll at 3 Pauls of yearly tribute; and of this class there are about 60. The 4 auzeani are also chosen by lot from the second class, who stand assessed at 2 Pauls. The spiritual government belongs to the bishop of Massa in the Maremma. The governor-general resides at Piombino. History.] This island was held in early times by the Pisans. On the annexation of Pisa to Milan, E., with Piombino and some other territories, was formed into a little principality. In 1548, Charles V. annexed it to the duchy of Tuscany. From July 1796 to April 1797, it was held by England. The treaty of Amiens ceded it to France in 1802; but in 1807 it was reunited to Tuscany. The 9th art, of the treaty of Paris in 1814, erected it into "a separate principality," with a revenue of 6,000,000 francs, under the dethroned emperor of France, who resided here from 3d May, 1814, to 26th February, 1815, when he embarked with about 600 of his old guards, who had followed him to E., and landed on the 1st of March at Cannes. Authorities.] Castelli, Descrizione istoriografa dell' isola dell' Elba. Florence, 1814, 8vo.—T. de Berneaud's Voyage to the island of E. London, 1814 - Sir R. C. Hoare's Classical Tour. Lond., 1819, 2 vols. 8vo. - Williams's Travels. Edin., 1820, 2 vols. 8vo. -United Service Journal, 1822 -Bowring's Report on the Manufactures of Tuscany.—Valery, Voyage en Corse, a l'isle d'Elbe, &c. Paris, 1837, 2 vols. 8vo.

ELBA, a township of Genessee co., in the state of New York, U. S., 7 m. N of Batavia. It has an undulating surface, and is watered by Oak-Orchard creek. Its soil consists of sand and gravelly loam. Pop. in 1840, 3,161.—Also a township of Lapeer co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 101. ELBAH, ELBEH, or OLAKY (JEBEL), a range of mountains in Nubia, running parallel to the coast, between 21° 30' and 22° 30'. Its highest summit, near its S extremity, rises to the alt. of 2,300 ft. above sea-level. On the N it does not exceed 1,700 ft. in height. Gold is said to be found in large quantities in this mountain. A ramification runs E, and forms a headland of the same name, on the Red sea.

EL-BASSAN, or ALBASANO, a sanjak and town of Turkey in Europe, in Albania. The sanj. is 30 m. in its greatest length from E to W, and about 24 m. in breadth from N to S. It is generally mountainous, and is intersected in a W direction by the Scombi. The town is situated in a fertile plain, near the interior of the sanj., on a small river of the same name, an affluent of the Scombi, 30 m. NNE of Berat, and 65 m. SSE of Scutari. Pop. 5,000. which forms the residence of the governor. Iron and copper wares are extensively manufactured here. Rice and olives are cultivated in the environs.

ELBE, a large river of Europe, the Albis of the ancients, and the LABE of the Bohemians, whose course lies entirely within German territory, watering in succession the plains of Bohemia, Saxony, Prussia, Mecklenburg, Hanover, and Denmark. It takes its rise near the sources of the Oder and the Vistula, at the confluence of the Weiswasserbach and the Elbbach, in that part of the Carpathian mountains known as the Riesengebirge, on the W slope of the Schneekoppe, at an alt. of 4,500 ft. above sealevel. In its course through Bohemia it receives several tributary streams, but particularly the Moldau, above Melnik. At Leitmeritz it receives the Eger from Bavaria, at a point where its alt. above sealevel is 366 ft. After leaving Bohemia, at a narrow pass at Winterberg, near Schandau-which Werner conceives to have been the outlet of a great lake which once covered the whole of Bohemia-it enters Saxony at an alt. of about 338 ft. above sea-level, passes Dresden, and receives the Mülde not far from Dessau. After receiving the Saale above Barby, and the Havel near Havelberg-which latter river forms a communication with the Oder by means of the Spree -it pursues its way through the confines of the duchies of Lunenburg and Mecklenburg-Schwerin, where it receives the Ilmenau. It then advances to Hamburg, where its branches form several islands. Below Glukstadt it receives the Stör; and at the distance of 85 m. from this point, it throws itself into the North sea, not far from Heligoland, after a course, in direct distance from its source, of 400 m., or of 650 m. by its windings. Its principal tributaries on the r. are the Cydlina, Iser, Polzen, Schwarz- Elster, Havel, Elde, Stockenitz, and Stör; on the l., the Adler, Modlau, Eger, Mulde, Saale, Jeetze, Ilmenau, and Oste. It drains an area of about 60,000 sq. m. Its mean depth is said to be about 9 ft.; and its average breadth, 900 ft. Yet, upon the whole, this river, though carrying a prodigious body of water into the North sea, affords but indifferent facilities to navigation. The navigation of the E. from Cuxhaven up to Hamburg, is difficult on account of its numerous sand - banks, which render the navigable channel both intricate and narrow. When a violent wind blows from the W in autumn, its waters are swollen to such a degree that the canals of Hamburg overflow their banks, fill the cellars and magazines with water, and often inundate the streets. An E wind produces an opposite effect: driving the waters of the E, towards the sea with so much force that the canals are left dry, and passengers cross them on foot. The E. is navigable for vessels drawing 14 ft. water up to Hamburg; and light steamers now ascend it as far as Melnik in Bohemia. It forms the principal channel of communication with the North sea to the whole N of Germany. It is connected by canals with the Oder and the Trave. The free navigation of the river, however, is greatly injured by the excessive number of tolls and restrictions imposed by the sovereigns of the different territories through which it flows. Thus, previous to arrangements made in March 1822, merchandise sent from Pirna, in Saxony, was formally obliged to pay 32 tolls before it reached Hamburg; and at Magdeburg all vessels not under Prussian colours, or carrying Prussian produce, were compelled to unload, and ship their goods in Prussian vessels. The Stade-dues on the E. were instituted in 1038 by Conrad II., in favour of the church at Hamburg, which had suffered very severely at the hands of the heathenish Slawen. It existed still when Hanover, in 1715, bought the ELB whole territory from Sweden. The duty, which then amounted to 1-16 per cent. on all goods passing up the E., had been recognised by Hamburg in 1692, but no sooner had Stade passed into the possession of Hanover than several very oppressive regulations were enacted in spite of all the protestations of Hamburg. In 1804, when the French occupied Hanover, the duty was momentarily abolished; but in 1814, when they had been driven out of N. Germany, it was re-established and increased. In 1815 the matter was brought before the congress at Vienna, and resolutions were adopted for abolishing all burdens on the navigation of the E., and regulating the conditions on which the navigation was to be thrown open. In 1819 a commission was opened at Dresden to deliberate upon the means of carrying out these resolutions. But Hanover declared that the Stadedues were not touched by the resolutions, since they were not river but sea-dues. It is well known that sea-dues are only levied by pirates; but the commission was satisfied with this declaration, and only required a promise that the dues should not be increased. The promise was given, but not acted upon; for the dues were raised very considerably, and the new tariff was not even laid before the commission, which concluded its labours in 1821. In 1824 a new commission was appointed. The Holstein commissioner proved historically that the Stadedues had always been river-dues, and could not be sea-dues, and the representative of Hamburg produced the tariffs of 1692, 1814, and 1821, and showed that the duties had been raised from 1-16th to 1, 13. and 3 per cent. on many articles. Hanover, however, maintained that she was justified in her proceedings, and the second commission, as well as those in 1827 and 1842, were without effect. The E. dues, which are raised in addition by Hanover, Austria, Prussia, Saxony, Anhalt, Mecklenburg, and Denmark, amount to 1 thaler on every cwt. What are called "Recognition dues," amounting from 3 to 13 thalers, according to the size of the vessels, are paid to 14 different states through which the E. flows!
ELBE-KOSTELETZ, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Kaurzim, 13 m. NE of Prague, on the l. bank of the Elbe. Pop. 900.

ELBEN, a village of Kur-Hessen, in the circle and 8 m. NNW of Fritzlar, and 17 m. WSW of Cassel, on the l. bank of the Little Elbe. It has a castle, and contains the largest paper-mill in the duchy. Pop. 580.

ELBERFELD, or ELBERVELDE, a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, regency of Dusseldorf, in a fine valley, on the Wüpper, at an alt. of 450 ft. above sea-level, and 16 m. E of Dusseldorf, with which it is connected by railroad. Pop. in 1837, 26,770; in 1846, 35,100. It consists of two parts, distinguished as the Island and the Freiheit; and contains a Catholic and two Protestant churches, a gymnasium, a school of arts, a school for the deaf and dumb, a bank, a trades'-house, a savings'-bank, numerous burgher-schools, &c. The modern parts of the town are well-built, and paved and lighted with gas, but in its greater extent it is straggling and irregular, stretching along the Wüpper upwards to Barmen. E, is noted for its silk and cotton manufactures, and forms one of the most important seats of industrial activity in Germany. It has also manufactures of velvet, ribbon, tape, lace, hosiery, parchment, steel, and iron-mongery; and in the environs are numerous dye-works and bleachfields. The factories are nearly all worked by water-power, and are consequently scattered along the course of the Wüpper. E. is the entrepot for all the productions of the local industry of the surrounding district, and has a considerable foreign trade.—The circle of E, comprises * the valley of Barmen, and contains 4 towns, and 12 villages. Pop. in 1842, 104,532, or nearly 940 per sq. m.

ELBERT, a county of the state of Georgia, U.S., comprising an area of 560 sq. m., bordered on the NE by the Savannah river, and on the SW by Broad river. It is generally hilly, but in the S possesses a fine loamy soil. Pop. in 1840, 11,125. Its capital, Elberton, is 111 m. NNE of Milledgeville.

ELBE-TEINITZ, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 21 m. WNW of Chrudim, and 45 m. E of Prague, on the r. bank of the Elbe, and on the Prague and Olmutz railway. Its inhabitants, numbering about 1,830, employ themselves chiefly in agriculture and fishing. Limestone is found in the environs.

ELBEUF, or ELBEUF, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Rouen.—The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831. 21,262; in 1841, 27,984.—The town is 12 m. SSW of Rouen, on the l. bank of the Seine, and is intersected by the Puchot, the waters of which are noted for their purity. By railway, from the Tourville station of the Paris and Havre railroad, it is 773 m. from Paris, and 66 m. from Havre. Pop. in 1831, 10,258; in 1846, 16,010. It is noted for its manufacture of cloth, and has numerous spinningmills, dye-works, bleachfields, saw-mills, and manufactories of soap and chemical substances; carding and other machines are also extensively manufactured here. It is the entrepot for the cloth of Sedan and Louviers, the wool of Spain, Italy, Germany, and England, and has an active trade in grain and wood. Four markets are held here weekly, and fairs twice a-year. Daily communication is maintained with Rouen by means of steam-boats. E. is one of the most ancient manufacturing towns in France; so early as the middle of the 16th cent. it could number about 80 manufactural establishments.

ELBING, a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. of W. Prussia, regency and 34 m. ESE of Dantzig, on a river of the same name, which issues from Lake Drausen, communicates with the Nogat by the canal of Kraffohl, and falls into the Frischehaff, after a course of 18 m. Pop. in 1837, 18,725; in 1846, 20,000. It consists of an old and new town, and numerous suburbs; and is surrounded by walls flanked with old towers and ditches. The streets of the old town are narrow, and the houses lofty; in the new the streets are spacious, well-built, and well-lighted with gas. The town has, upon the whole, a neat and fashionable appearance, with a bustling pop. The principal public buildings are the churches, the synagogue, the gymnasium, the bank, the hospitals, the orphan's asylum, the houseof-correction, the house of industry, &c. It has extensive manufactories of sugar, potash, chicory, soap, vitriol, tobacco, oil, starch, woollen fabrics, &c., and building-docks; and its trade is extremely active. Its exports consist chiefly in soap, potash, grain, butter, wood, wool, feathers, hair, and silk; and its imports in colonial grain and wine. The port is situated at the junction of a canal from the Frische-haff with the Elbing, and is capable of receiving small vessels; larger vessels discharge at the mouth of the E., whence their cargoes are conveyed up the river by barges or lighters suited to its navigation.-The circle of E. had a pop. in 1842 of 40,108.

ELBINGERODE, a well-built town of Hanover, on the Rohbach, in the landr. of Hildesheim, 6 m. E of Goslar, at an alt. of 1,500 ft. above sea-level. It was built in its present improved style after a great fire in 1752; and contains a pop. of 3,050, who are chiefly employed in mining, iron-forging, and brewing. In the neighbourhood are the large iron-mines of Rothehütte. ELB ELBŒUF. See ELBEUF.

ELBOGEN, or Ellenbogen, a circle and town of Bohemia.—The circle is a mountainous district on the W side of the kingdom, with an area of 1,186 sq. m., and a pop. in 1816, of 188,427; in 1837, 248,341. Its principal streams are the Eger, the Tepl, the Striela, the Zwoda, and the Weistritz. Mining employs a large proportion of the pop.—The town stands on the l. bank of the Eger, at an alt. of 1,324 ft. above sea-level, 80 m. W of Prague. It has extensive porcelain manufactories.
ELBRIDGE, a township in the state of New York, U. S., 13 m. W of Syracuse. Pop. 4,647.
ELBUGA, a river of Russia, which runs into the Kagalinsk, 10 m. E of Azof.

ELBURG, a small town of Holland, in the prov. of Gueldreland, on the E coast of the Zuyder-Zee. 34 m. NNE of Utrecht. It contains 2,100 inhabitants, chiefly employed in fishing. ELBURZ CHAIN. See article Asia, p. 365. ELBURZ, ELBROUTZ, ELBROOZ, or ELBORUS (Mount), a lofty summit in the central chain of the Caucasian system, to the N of N lat. 43°, and W of E long. 42°, 21 m. N of Kotaïs, and 150 m. NW of Tifles. The ascent of Mount E. was performed in July 1829 by General Emmanouel, Professor Kupffer of Casan, M. Zenz, as physical observer, M. Menetrier, zoologist, and M. Meyer of Dorpat, botanist. The party was guarded by 600 infantry, 350 Cossacks, and 2 cannons; and their baggage and instruments were carried by 6 camels and several carriages. They reported that the plateau upon which Mount E. stands is from 8,000 to 10,000 ft. high, stretching E and W; that it is torn up in all directions by narrow and deep valleys; and is crossed in its middle, from E to W, by a crest of rugged rocks of picturesque character, whose summits are covered with eternal snow. On this crest, and nearly in the middle of its length, there occurs a large and deep excavation, the middle of which is occupied by a cone which might have been supposed to be entirely covered with snow, but for here and there the naked rocks appearing through it: this cone is Mount E., whose height exceeds by 3,000 or 4,000 ft. all the surrounding mountains. The party passed the night at the foot of the cone in a small hollow, sheltered by enormous blocks of black porphyry with white spots, and in the middle of which was a small pool of snow water, but which presented no trace of verdure, and only a few lichens on the bare rocks. Next morning, the 22d of July, the party rose at 3 o'clock. The therm. was at 30° Fahr. and the sky clear. They soon got upon the snow, and experienced all the difficulties and debilities so often described in accounts of similar ascents. Towards its summit, Mount E. presents a series of naked rocks forming a species of stairs which greatly facilitates the ascent. MM. Kupffer, Menetrier, and Meyer, were so exhausted, that they resolved to rest for some hours; but during this delay the snow became so soft under the heat of the sun, that it became necessary to return, lest a bridge of snow by which they had crossed a chasm should be melted. M. Zenz who had gone on, reached the last platform of rocks, and found himself removed from the summit only by an interval of snow. The causes which rendered the return of the party necessary prevented them from advancing, and out of 15 or 20 persons, Cossacks and Circassians, who attempted to reach the summit, only one succeeded, viz., a Circassian named Krillar, who, inspired by a reward which General Emmanouel had offered, set off very early, and availed himself of the morning's frost. The descent was extremely difficult, from the cause already mentioned; but at 7 o'clock in the evening they Zenz obtained the following results:- French feet. Height of the mineral springs of Konstantirogoork, 1,300 - of the limit of snow, . . . M. Oichnevski had formerly estimated its elevation at <math>2,783</math> toises = <math>16,038</math> ft.—The temp. of the air at the limit of snow was 9° 6' Reaum., or about 54° Fahr. At the station of M. Zenz it was 1° 5' R., or 35½ F.; while at the mineral springs it was 23° R., or 81° F., at the time of the first observation; and 24° R., or 86° F., at the second. The first of these observations gives 680 ft. of difference of level for each octogesimal degree, the second, only 630 ft. One of the most interesting results obtained by the expedition was a magnetic one. It was found that the magnetic intensity decreased 0,"01 upon 24" for every 1,000 ft. of elevation,—a result which M. Kupffer considered incompatible with the hypothesis of a magnetic nucleus.

ELCHE, a considerable town of Spain, in the prov. and 13 m. SW of Alicant. It is situated near the l. bank of the Elda, in a plain, covered in a great measure with date-palm trees; and has altogether an Oriental appearance. Pop. in 1845, 18,068. It is and having still a ducal palace evidently the erection of a remote age. It has several good streets and squares, but the general aspect of the place is melancholy. The great church is a beautiful building, with a majestic dome; the barracks are also extensive and well-built. There are several convents. The manufactures are confined to soap and leather; the trade of the place, such as it is, consists in these articles, and in dates, palm-branches which are exported to various places for the church-festivals, and agricultural produce. The town is supplied with water from public fountains, several of which are of marble.—Also a small town in the prov. and 65 m. SSW of Albacete, and 27 m. SE of Alcaraz, on the Segura.

ELCHINGEN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, district of Guntzburg, on the l. bank of the Danube, and 7 m. below Ulm. It was from a successful exploit against the Austrians near this, on 14th October, 1805, that Ney received from Bonaparte the title of duke of E.
ELCI, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 24 m. SW of Sienna, on the Cecina. Pop. 174.

ELDA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. WNW of Alicant, situated on the l. bank of the Elda, near the foot of the Sierra-de-Camara. The streets are, with few exceptions, narrow, dirty, and without pavement, and the houses small and illbuilt. Pop. 3,846. It has manufactures of soap, leather, and brandy.—The river rises in a lake 6 m. NE of Villena, and runs from NNW to SSE to Lake Elche, on the SE of the town of that name.
ELDAGSEN, a small town of Hanover, in the principality of Kalenberg, 13 m. SW of Hanover, on the Gehlenbicke. Pop. 2,210.

ELDE, a river of Germany, in the duchy of Mecklenburg-Schwerin, which takes its rise in the lake of Müritz, and falls into the Elbe on the r. bank, near Dömitz. Its principal affluents are the Stör on the r., and the Löcknitz on the l. It communicates by the Müritz lake with the Kavel.
ELDENA, a town of Prussian Pomerania, in the circle and 5 m. E of Griefswalde, on the Stör. Pop. 1,540. There is a school-of-agriculture here, founded in 1835.
ELDERSFIELD, a parish of Worcestershire, ELE 186 reached their camp on the banks of the Malka. M. | 6 m. SW of Tewkesbury. Area 3,400 acres. Pop. 837.
ELDERSLIE, a village in the co. of Renfrew, 2 m. NE of Paisley. Pop. 1,086, chiefly weavers and Upha.
ELDON, a township in the p. of St. Andrew- Auckland, in Durham, 3 m. SE of Auckland. Pop. 186.—Also a township in Colborne district, in Upper Canada, between Fenelon and Thorah townships.
ELDUAYEN, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. S of Saint Sebastian, near the r. bank of the Berastegui.
ELDVATN, a small stream on the S coast of Iceland, falling into the sea in N lat. 63° 17'.
ELE. See ILI.
ELEANOR (Point), the N extremity of Knight's island, in Prince William's sound, in N lat. 60° 37'.
ELEANOR'S COVE, a harbour on the W coast of N. America, on the E side of Behring's bay, in N lat. 59° 44'.
ELEIGH, a parish in Suffolk, 5 m. NW of Hadleigh. Area 1,670 acres. Pop. 732.
ELEK (SAINT), a town of Hungary, in the com. of Eisenburg, 28 m. SW of Güns.
ELENA (Point Santa), a long strip of land endof great antiquity, being the Illici of the Romans, ing in a cape on the coast of Ecuador, in S lat. 2° 12', W long. 81°.

ELENA (PORT SANTA), a bay and harbour on the coast of Patagonia, in S lat. 44° 30′ 45", W long. 65° 17' 25". The tide sets strong along the shore here, which is fronted by reefs. The coast is dry and bare of vegetation, and a long extent of treeless undulating plain stretches inward to the base of a ridge of a range of high flat-topped hills. The geological structure of the coast consists of a fine-grained porphyritic clay-slate. Guanacoes, ostriches, armadillos, and the cavia or Patagonian hare, besides wild ducks, partridges, snipes, and rails, are to be procured here.
ELENEA, a commune and village of Belgium, in E. Flanders, 12 m. E of Oudenarde. Pop. 649.

ELEPHANTA, called by the natives GHARIPUR, a small island in the harbour and 7 m. SW of Bombay. It is composed of two hills with a narrow valley between them, and is about 4 m. in circumf., and contains about 100 inhabitants, who cultivate rice, but are principally employed in rearing sheep and poultry for the Bombay market. It is much overgrown with wood, and contains several springs of good water. It owes its celebrity to its wonderful cave and mythological sculptures. Opposite to the landing-place stood a colossal figure of an elephant, now fallen into decay, from which the Portuguese named the island. A short distance from this is the entrance to a cave nearly 130 ft. long, 123 ft. broad, and 18 ft. high, supported by 26 pillars cut out of the rock, in the sides of which there are numerous compartments, containing various representations of Hindu deities. In the centre of the excavation there is a remarkable bust which Forbes supposes to represent the Hindu triad: viz., Brahma the Creator, Vishnu the Preserver, and Seva or Mahadeva the Destroyer; but recent researches have shown that Seva himself is represented by this tri-fronted image. The heads, which are six feet in length, are well cut; and the countenances, with the exception of the under lip, handsome. On each side is a pilaster, the front of which is filled up by a human figure leaning on a dwarf. Beside the principal apartment, there are several other small chambers, containing various mythological representations. This place of worship is said to have been dedicated to Seva, but has long been forsaken by priests or Brahmins. The rock out of which it is carved is soft, and rapidly decomposes under the rains; it is hardly reasonable to suppose, therefore, that this cave temple is very ancient.
ELEPHANT CAYS, a group of low sandy islets surrounded by reefs, off Speedwell island, in the Falkland group.
ELEPHANT MOUNTAIN, a mountain on the SE coast of Ceylon, 74 m. SE of Candy.
ELEPHANT (POINT), the extremity of the narrow peninsula that bounds the E and SE sides of the harbour of Trincomalee, in the island of Ceylon. Near it, on the SE side, is an island called E. island.

ELEPHANT'S RIVER, or OLIFANT, a large river in the Clanwilliam district of Cape Colony, which rises in the Tulbagh mountains, and empties itself into the Atlantic, in S lat. 31° 30', after a course of about 160 m. It is navigable for small craft to the distance of 20 m.

ELEPHANTINA, or ELEPHANTINE', [ARAB. Jeziret-el-Zahir or el-Sag], a small island in the Nile, opposite to Assuan, in Upper Egypt, in N lat. 20° 5' 2", remarkable for the ruins with which it is covered. It is 3,000 ft. long, and from 400 to 900 ft. broad. The S part is elevated, rocky, and barren; the N part is low, and covered with rich doura fields, date-groves, pastures, and huts. Some travellers represent this island as exhibiting a scene of Elysian beauty; others pronounce it to be little better than | duchy and 29 m. SSE of Nassau, capital of the dis- "a mountain of brick-bats, broken pottery, and sand." The stream of the Nile, for about a mile above, is interrupted by numerous small rocks of that fine red granite which characterises the ruins on E., and which produced so many portals, columns, and obelisks, to adorn the chief cities of antiquity. The whole channel of the river is broken by a thousand islets of various forms, some rugged, black, and bare; others adorned with beautiful verdure, reeds, and tamarisks. It is believed that there was once a great temple on this island dedicated to the god Knuphis, all traces of which are entirely obliterated; but there still remains a large pyramidal portal of red granite which is supposed to have formed the entrance to it. Two temples were demolished by order of Mehemet Ali for the sake of their materials to construct barracks for his black regiments at Assuan. The island in general is covered with heaps of ruins piled upon each other, and exhibiting various styles of architecture, Egyptian, Roman, Saracen, and Arab. On the E side, there are remains of a high wall, constructed perpendicularly above the water, to support the soil of the island against the force of the stream. The masonry of this work is admirable, though it is difficult to discover the period to which it belongs. The inhabitants of E. are Nubians, who differ greatly in complexion, features, and language from the Egyptians on the opposite shore.
ELERSI, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Diarbekir, 75 m. S of Kirkisieh.
ELESCHEVI, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, on the river Tchulim, 84 m. ENE of Tomsk.
ELESD, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Bihar, on the Schnelle-Körös, 26 m. ESE of Bihar.
ELETUR, a town of Hindostan, in the circar of Guntur, 24 m. W of Masulipatam.

ELETZ, or YELETZ, a town of European Russia, in the gov. of Orel, situated on a hill in a beautiful plain between the river whence its name has been derived, and the Sossna, both branches of the Don, 115 m. ESE of Orel. It maintains a considerable commerce in cattle, corn, leather, and honey, and is renowned for its iron forges which are supplied with ore from the neighbourhood.
ELEVONEAH, a town of Hindostan, in Mysore, 10 m. NNE of Bangalore.

ELEUSINA, or LEPSINA, a village of Greece, in ELG the dep. of Attica, 12 m. NW of Athens, adjacent to a beautiful plain, and occupying the site of the once famous city of E., whose ruins are still considerable, consisting of six arches of an aqueduct, the fragments of a temple of Ceres, of a temple of Neptune, of a theatre, and part of a paved road crossing the plain to the E, the remains of the old causeway along which the procession was wont to move from Athens to E. The present v. does not contain above 40 "miserable mud houses."

ELEUTHERA, or ALABASTER ISLAND, one of the largest of the Bahama islands, situated on the most eastern part of the Great Bahama bank, between N lat. 24° 39' and 25° 31', and W long. 76° 22' and 76° 56', 50 m. NE of New Providence. Its E shore is washed by the blue and fathomless ocean; on the W it has the white, shallow, smooth water of the bank. Its surface has an average elevation above the sea of 80 ft. It is 80 m. in length, and 10 m. in greatest breadth; but its area is estimated at only 260 sq. m. Pop. in 1842, 3,886. Upon the W side of the island is the settlement of Wreck-sound; but the largest settlement is on the N extremity of the island.
ELEVEN POINTS, a river of the state of Missouri, U. S., which unites with the Big Black river, 5 m. NE of Davidsonville.

ELFELD, or ELTVILLE, a bail. and town in the trict of the Rheingau, on the Rhine. Pop. 2,234. It is well built, and has a castle and a church. Wine forms its chief article of trade. The bail. contains 9,714 inhabitants, of whom the majority are Catholics.

ELFKARLEBY, a town of Sweden, in the laen and 50 m. N of Upsala, on the l. bank of the Elf- Dal, near the confluence of that river with the gulf of Bothnia. It has several iron works and extensive fisheries.
ELFORD, a parish of Staffordshire, 4½ m. NNW of Tamworth. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 434.—Also a township in the p. of Bamborough, Northumberland. Pop. 112.
ELFSBORG. See Wenersborg.
ELFSBORG, or ELVESBORG (NY), a fortress of Sweden, in the laen and 5 m. W of Göttenburg, on an island of the Cattegat, at the mouth of the Göta. It was built in 1646.
ELFVEDAL, or ELF-DAL, a parish and village of Sweden, in the laen and 75 m. NW of Falun, on the l. bank of the Dal. Pop. 2,700. In the environs are extensive iron-works and quarries of porphyry.
ELGAR, one of the Orkney islands, in the p. and about a furlong to the S of Shapinsay.

ELGERSBURG, a seignory and village of Saxe- Coburg-Gotha, 21 m. SSE of Gotha. Pop. 640. It has a fine castle, and possesses considerable manufactories of delf-ware, draining pipes, articles in wood, potash, and pitch. Coal is wrought in the environs.—The seignory contains 5 villages, and in 1842 had 2,047 inhabitants.

ELGG, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 18 m. NE of Zurich, pleasantly situated on the Eulach. Pop. 3,000. It has an ancient castle. Glass is manufactured here, and in the environs is a mine of coal.

ELGIN, a parish and town in the county of Elgin. The p. extends about 10 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth. Pop., including the burgh of E., in 1801, 4,345; in 1831, 6,130; in 1841, 6,083. The surface is flat. The vales of Mosstowie and Pluscardine are separated by a steep hilly ridge; and the district rises gently from the vicinity of the town towards the Black-hills. The only river of any importance is the Lossie, to which a tributary stream runs from the Black-hills. In a secluded glen, at the W end of the p., stand the fine ruins of Pluscardine abbey. The ridge between Mosstowie and Pluscardine consists of strata of a hard and pale-coloured sandstone; the old red sandstone also appears in this district.

ELGIN, the cap. of the co. of Moray or Elgin, once the see and cathedral seat of the great bishopric of Moray, is situated 144 m. N of Edinburgh; 63 m. NW of Aberdeen; and 5 m. S of Lossiemouth, its sea-port. It extends nearly 1 m. along the S bank of the Lossie, in the midst of very pleasing scenery. Many of the houses of the long main street of E., as it existed at the commencement of the present cent., were of venerable age, with high-crowned roofs, overlaid with heavy slabs, and presenting to the street the portly fore-stair, and a double range of open piazza, consisting of a series of pillared arches in the front wall, over the entrance to a paved and sheltered court within; but few of these piazzas now exist, and some that do, are either built entirely up, or otherwise converted into shops of modern style. -St. Giles's, or 'the Muckle kirk,' was razed, in 1826, to make way for the present splendid substitute. A new and elegant court-house has been erected in the market-place; and immediately behind, a new prison.—The cathedral, in the days of its perfection, was the most splendid specimen of ecclesiastical architecture in Scotland, the abbeychurch of Melrose not excepted. This great religious foundation owed its origin to Bishop Andrew Moray, who is said to have founded it on the site of an old church in 1224. But after standing 166 years, the original fabric was destroyed in June 1390, by Alexander Stuart, son of Robert II., usually called the Wolf of Badenoch. Bishop Barr began soon after to rebuild it, but many painful years were spent, together with a third of all the revenues of the bishops, ere that one dark day's disaster was repaired. From 1538, the fabric continued in a state of perfect preservation till the Reformation, ten years after which, in 1568, the privy-council actually issued its orders, "to take the lead from the cathedralchurches of Aberdeen and E., and sell the same" for the maintenance of Regent Murray's soldiers! Since that period, the cathedral of E., unprotected from the weather, has gradually gone to ruin and destruction. Still, its splendid ruins amply justify even the highest estimate of its original magnificence, and constitute the chief amid the numerous attractions of this limited but interesting city; and government has latterly caused much attention to be paid in clearing out the ruins of this and other of our Scottish cathedrals, and in preventing them from falling into complete decay. Like all similar fabrics of its time, the cathedral of E. stood due E and W, and was built in the form of a Jerusalem or Passion cross. The great tower fell in 1711. The dimensions of the cathedral given in the New Statistical Account are as follow: Length of cathedral over walls, 264 ft.; breadth, 35 ft.; traverse, 114 ft.; height of centre tower, 198 ft.; eastern turrets, 60 ft.; western towers without the spires, 84 ft.; side wall, 36 ft. The chapter-house, attached to the N cloister of the cathedral, is extremely elegant. It is an octagon, with a pillar of elaborate workmanship in the centre, supporting a richly groined roof. The original seat of the bishopric was at Spynie; and indeed, prior to the 13th cent., the bishop transferred his chair from one church to another as suited his convenience; but, at the request of the chapter, and of King Alexander II., it was translated to Elgin, in virtue of a bull from Pope Honorius, dated 10th April, 1224. The diocese was a very extensive one, comprehending the whole of the present cos. of Elgin and Nairn, and also part of Aberdeen, Banff, and Inverness.-E.'s ancient glory has departed with its princely bishopric, and gorgeous religious rites; 188 ELG but it has kept pace with the modern march of human progress. New and very handsome houses occupy the places of the old. New streets have even started up; and villas, built in an elegant style, and interspersed with shrubberies and gardens, now adorn the S suburbs. The streets are lighted with gas, and the town is now well-drained and cleaned. The new church is one of the most elegant structures in the N of Scotland. It has a spacious portico of Doric columns covering its W entrance, and a handsome tower with clock and bells, surmounted by a lantern and copula. Trinity Episcopal chapel has a handsome Gothic front, and, besides a Roman Catholic chapel, there are two for congregations of Seceders, and one for Independents. Assembly-rooms were erected and tastefully fitted up in 1822. There is an excellent public library in the town, a museum, and an academy of considerable standing and repute. The E. Institution was founded and endowed in 1832, from funds amounting to £70,000, bequeathed for the maintenance of aged men and women, and the maintenance and education of poor or orphan boys and girls, by General Anderson. It is a quadrangular building of two stories, surmounted by a circular tower and dome. The institution for the children contains a school-of-industry; the children are apprenticed also to some trade or useful occupation. Gray's hospital constitutes another beneficent institution also founded by a native of E., Dr. Gray, who was afterwards resident at Calcutta. It is intended for relief of the sick poor of the town and county of Elgin, and was founded and endowed from a bequest of £26,000. The building was erected in 1819, on a slight but spacious eminence at the W end of the town. Its situation is well chosen, and being a handsome edifice, in the Grecian style, with a projecting portico of Doric columns, it forms a splendid termination to the High-street.—A small Lunatic asylum for paupers has been lately erected on the hospital grounds.—The pop. of the burgh in 1831 was 4,493; in 1841, 3,911. It is classed with Banff, Cullen, Inverury, Kintore, and Peterhead, in returning one member to parliament. The parliamentary constituency, in 1839, was 249; in 1848, 238. The revenue of the burgh is about £500.—E. gives the title of Earl to a branch of the royal house of Bruce. Thomas, third Lord Bruce of Kinross, was created Earl of E. in 1633.
ELGIN, a village of Kane co., in the state of Illinois, U. S., 199 m. NNE of Springfield. Pop. in 1840, 300.

ELGINSHIRE, or Morayshire, the central division of the ancient prov. of Moray in Scotland, is bounded on the N by the Moray frith; on the E and SE by Banffshire; on the S by Inverness-shire; and on the W by Inverness-shire and Banffshire. Over 25 m. its E boundary is traced by the river Spey; and over 24 m. on the W it is formed by a ridgy water-shed, the NE prolongation of the Monadleagh mountains; everywhere else, except along the Moray frith, the boundaries are altogether artificial. Even the Spey is a boundary-line only at intervals, or with interruptions. The county itself consists of two separate though not widely detached parts. The larger part lies on the N; and is not very far from having the outline of an equilateral triangle. The smaller division is separated from the larger by the intervention of a detached district of Inverness-shire, and by two small detached districts of Nairnshire; and lies to the SW at the distance of from 31/2 to 5 m. The superficial area of the co. is, in all authorities, so very variously and loosely stated, that it seems never to have been even proximately ascertained. The Edinburgh Almanac states the area at 840 sq. m., or 537,600 acres, of which 120,000 are cultivated, 200,000 uncultivated, and 217,600 unprofitable. Physical features.] The sea-board of the greater of the two detached districts of the co. is, in a general view, a champaign country. Its breadth ranges between 5 and 12 m., and probably averages about 7 m. A pendicle of it in the NE consists of the greater portion of the Culbin sands. The district in the extreme S, called Brae-Moray, differs from the central mountain-ground chiefly in being extensively occupied with thriving forests of natural pine. Though the champaign district has a N exposure, and lies within the 58th parallel of N lat., it possesses singular mildness of climate. The wind blows from some point near the W during about 260 days in the year. Winter is here singularly mild, and only for a brief aggregate period sheets the ground with snow; but summer rarely commences its characteristic warmth till July.—The Spey, with the aid of its tributaries, drains rather more than a moiety of the whole area of the county. Dulnainwater, coming in from Inverness-shire, and running to the Spey, cuts the W and larger section of Brae- Moray into nearly equal parts. Nethy-water drains most of the E section of Brae-Moray. Other tributaries of the Spey in the co. are very numerous; but in no instance have more than about 6 m. length to run. The Lossie rises and has its whole course in the co.; and makes a long curving sweep through nearly the centre of the lowlands, washing in its progress the site of the town of E. The Findhorn, the most scenic stream of all Moray, and rich in the number and variety of its landscapes, runs 11 m. in the extreme W of the co.—The loch of Spynie, situated in the cognominal parish, was formerly a conspicuous lake of 3 m. in length, but is now much reduced by draining. Loch Cots, in the same neighbourhood, was originally marine, and, in the 13th cent., is described as a bay.—Sandstone occurs in great plenty and excellence; and, in various localities, is quarried for every purpose of architecture, and for pillars, rollers, and paving-flags. Limestone is worked in various quarters. Coal is stored up by conjecture under the unexplored depths of the sandstone beds and the harder layers of the limestone strata; but has often baffled search. Lead was mined for to the W of Lossie-mouth, about the year 1773; but it occurred only in small nodules. Iron ore is believed to exist in abundance in the high country; and about the year 1730, an iron foundery, once of high consideration, but long ago almost forgotten, was established by the York building-company at Coulnacoil in Strathspey. Several schistose rocks, so laminated that they form slabs or slates, occur among the primitive formations of the uplands. A stone, of a purple colour, highly indurated by a ferruginous matter, admitting the smoothest polish, but in a slight degree diaphanous, occurs about Rothes on the Spey. and has, under the name of porphyry, been formed upon the lapidary's wheel into toys and trinkets. Soil and agriculture.] The cultivated soil of the co. may be classified under the general names of sand, clay, loam, and reclaimed moss. If all the arable grounds were distributed into 63 parts, the sandy soils would be found to cover 24 of these, the clay soils 11, the loam soils 27, and the reclaimed moss soils 1.—Leslie, in his Agricultural View of the Counties of Nairn and Moray, published in 1811, estimates the amount of waste grounds in the two cos. at 301,680 Scottish acres, in a supposed total of 407,200.—Forests of natural wood anciently covered the greater part of the champaign country, and formed part of the Sylva Caledonia of early historians. A forest of natural pine, on the Strathspey frontier | <del>*</del> toward Inverness-shire, and partly within that co., covers an area of nearly 20,000 acres; and has long supplied, by the floating down of its thinnings to Garmouth, an article of large and advantageous traffic. Other natural woods, consisting of birch, hazel, alder, and a few oaks, occur in little groves and stripes along the banks of the rivers.—Few farms comprehend 300 acres; a considerable number range between 300 and 140 acres; another considerable number range between 120 and 60; a number larger than that of both these classes range between 50 and 20; and some upon the skirts of the larger farms, or upon the sides and bottoms of the glens, range between 18 or 12 to 6 or 5 acres.-The valued rent of the county, in 1674, was £65,603 Scots. The real property, as assessed to property-tax in 1815, was £73,288; and in 1842-3, £99,298. Manufactures and trade.] Blankets and wearing apparel of wool and of flax, are, to a large extent, of home-manufacture in this co. Much flax is spun for the Aberdeen manufacturers; and some coarse woollen cloth, called plaiding, is made in private families. A manufactory of woollen cloth, which employs about 50 workmen, has long been established in St. Andrew's Lhanbryd. Tan-works have long existed in E. and Forres; and a small iron-foundery exists at Newmill. But the chief manufacture—one so extensive as even a dozen years ago to have annually paid £50,000 to government-is that of whisky.-Though black cattle and agricultural produce are the principal articles of export, there are some years in which no demand for cattle exists, and some in which little corn can be spared. Most of the grain exported is shipped at Findhorn, Lossiemouth, and Garmouth; and disposed of at Leith, Dundee, and London. Sheep and swine are generally purchased by the Aberdeen butchers. Pork and salted beef are shipped at the new harbour and village of Burgh-head. Timber, chiefly from Strathspey forest, is annually exported to the value of nearly £2,000; and salmon to the value of £3,000. The principal imports are flax, ropes, hard and soft soap, and foreign bar-iron. The fairs of the co. are in no instance considerable markets for sheep; and are less valued by the farmers than those of Banffshire. Roads and Railways.] One grand line of road runs along the champaign country through Lhanbryd, E., and Forres; and carries subordinate lines, aggregately amounting to about 70 m., running athwart the same district.—A great line passes up the Spey, but, over several m, is on the Banffshire side of the river. -Another line passes up the Findhorn and the Dorbac, to the former at Grantown in Inverness-shire. — One line connects E. and Rothes; and another diverges from Strathspey up the Dulmain -A full share of the speculative railway enterprise of 1845 was directed to this co. One projected line, 441 m. long, including branches, with a proposed capital of £300,000, passes from Inverness to E., through the towns of Nairn and Forres. The W end of this line, it was proposed, would be in connection with the Caledonian canal, while the E end would unite with the projected Aberdeen and E railway by Banff; and also with another projected and competing line, 76 m. in length, known as the Great North of Scotland line, and running from Aberdeen, up the valley of the Don, and by Huntly and Keith to E.-From the Inverness and E. line, branch-lines were projected to Lossiemouth and to Burgh-head harbours.-Another projected line, commencing at the bridge of Craigellachie, passes along the banks of the Spey, through Rothes, to the suspension-bridge, where it was intended it should join the Great North railway, already noticed, and be identified with it for 10 m to E., whence it would proceed in a direct line to the harbour of Lossiemouth. The total length of this line, known as the Morayshire line, is 21 m .- Another projected line, to intersect the S part of the co., was that from Perth to Inverness, 136 m. in length, passing Dunkeld, and proceeding through Athole and Strathspey, to the town of Nairn, where it would join the E. and Inverness line All these lines have shared the fate of other embryo projects of the season of their projection. Towns and General Statistics.] E. contains two royal burghs, Elgin and Forres; the small town of Fochabers, and the villages of Garmouth, Urquhart, Lossiemouth, Bishopmill, Findhorn, Rothes, Balnaton, and Burgh-head. Pop. in 1801, 26,705; in 1811, 28,108; in 1821, 31,162; in 1831, 34,231; in 1841, 35,012. Inhabited houses, in 1831, 6,919; families, 7,768. Houses in 1841, 8,154; but in most of the returns flats and outbuildings have been reckoned as distinct houses.—The number of paupers on the books of the kirk-session in 1836 was 970; in 1841, 1,037. In the year ending 14th May, 1847, the total number of poor on the roll was 1,591; and the money expended on their support, £6,954. In 1834 there were 21 parochial schools, attended by from 817 to 1,259 scholars; and 70 non-parochial schools, attended by from 2,609 to 3,159 scholars, within the co.—The co. unites with Nairnshire in sending a member to parliament. Constituency in 1839-40, 750; in 1842-3, 726; in 1848, 690.

ELGOIBAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Guipuzcoa, partido of Vergara, on the r. bank of the Deva, 22 m. WSW of San Sebastian. Pop. 2,035. It has a church, a convent, and an hospital, and possesses iron-works and foundries. In the environs are several mineral and thermal springs.
ELGORAB, or DOOHARAB, a small island in the Red sea, near the coast of Yemen, 54 m. NW of Loheia, in N lat. 16° 10'.
ELGUETA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Guipuzcoa, partido of Vergara, 30 m. SW of San Sebastian. Pop. 1,991. It has several churches, and an hospital.
ELHAM, a parish of Kent, 61 m. NNW of Folkstone. Area 6,240 acres. Pop. 1,291.
ELHENITZ, or LHENICE, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Prachin, 27 m. S of Pisek. Pop. 1,218.
ELIA (SANTA), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, 15 m. ENE of Campobasso. Pop. 3,120. Fairs are held twice a-year.
ELIAB, or Ealiab (AL), a town of Nubia, in the prov. and 50 m. NNE of Shendy, on the l. bank of the Nile, nearly opposite Hallawich.

ELIAS (SAINT), a summit of the island of Santa Maura, Ionian sea, 41 m. SW of Amaxichi. Alt. 3,000 ft.—Also a summit of the Morea, in the dio. of Lakonia, in the range of the Penta-Daktylon mountains, the ancient Taggetus, on the confines of the dioceses of Lakonia and Lacedæmonia, 10 m. SW of Mistra. Alt. 7,829 ft.—Also a summit of the island of Milo, Grecian archipelago.—Also a summit near the centre of the island of Paros.—Also a summit near the centre of the island of Zea.—Also a summit on the S side of the island of Ægina, near the SE coast.—Also a summit of the island of Scio, 15 m. NE of Scio.—Also a mountain of the island and 55 m. SE of Negropont. Alt. 4,607 ft. It is the ancient Ocho, and on its summit are the remains of an ancient Greek temple, supposed to have been dedicated to Neptune.—Also an islet near the SW coast of Cephalonia, in N lat. 38° 5'.

ELIAS (CAPE SAINT), a headland on the S coast of the island of Sardinia, separating the bay of Cagliaria from that of Quartu.—Also a headland of the Morea, at the SW extremity of the promontory which forms the E side of the gulf of Lakonia, to the W of Cape S. Angelo. About 3 m. N is a town of the same name.

ELIAS (Mount Saint), a remarkable volcanic mountain of N. America, under the 60th parallel, the principal summit of what has been named the cordillera of New Norfolk, and the highest peak in N. America. From the coast, which is partly composed of a spacious margin of low land, and from which this mountain is about 25 m. distant, the ground rises with a gradual and uniform ascent, into that lofty range of mountains which runs in a continued chain from the Frozen ocean through the whole continent of America. The summits of this chain form the base from whence Mount St. E. towers majestically conspicuous in regions of | long. 151° 3'. 190 ELI perpetual snow, to the enormous height of 16,760 ft above the level of the sea, or nearly the same height as Cotopaxi in S. America. On account of the great distance at which it is visible, Mount St. E. is a useful sea-mark to mariners when the sea is obscured by cloudy weather. Its position is taken by Raper as in N lat. 60° 18′, W long. 140° 52′. At the foot of Mount St. E., on the E, is Admiralty or Behring's bay; on the W side is Comptroller's bay, into which the Copper river flows.
ELIAS (POINT SAINT), a headland of the island of Ithaca, on the E coast, to the N of the gulf of Volo.
ELIE, or ELY, a parish and village of Fifeshire, on a bay of the firth of Forth, of the same name, 21 m. SSE of Colinsburgh. Area of p. 1,570 acres. Pop. 907. The v. has a good harbour. Pop. 829.
ELIENMARKT, a town of Transylvania, in the district of Siebenburgen, on the Maros, to the E of Dobra.
ELIKALPEIN BANK, a cluster of rocks in the Indian ocean, in the Lakadive archipelago, about 90 m. from the Malabar coast, in N lat. 11° 12'.
ELIMANE', or GEDINGUMA, a town of Sudan, in the kingdom of Kaarta, 60 m. NNW of Kemmu, and 300 m. NNE of Sego.
ELINCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Clary. Pop. in 1841, 1,539.
ELING, a parish of Hants, at the mouth of the Anton river, 4 m. WNW of Southampton. Area 14,950 acres. Pop. 5,410.
ELIS, or ILIA, a diocese or province and a town of Greece, in the Morea, on the NW coast. Pop. in 1839, 34,283. The town is 17 m. NW of Letrinoe, the capital of the prov.
ELISEO (Punta Santo), a summit of the island of Corsica, in the arrond. and 12 m. NE of Ajaccio. It rises to the height of 3,270 ft.

ELIZABETH, a township of Essex co., in the state of New Jersey, U.S., bordered on the E by Newark-bay and Staten-island, and watered by Bound and Morss brooks. Its soil is marshy, but generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 4,184.—Also a township, generally hilly, of Alleghany co., in the state of Pennsylvania, in the angle formed by Youghiogeny and Monongahela rivers, by the branches of which it is watered. Its soil is loamy, and it contains a considerable quantity of coal. Pop. 3,317.—Also a village of the last named township, on the E side of the Monongahela.—Also a township of Lancaster co., in the same state, bounded on the E by Trout, and on the W by Hammer creeks, and intersected by Middle creek, and its affluent Seglock creek. Its surface is hilly, and its soil red shale and gravel. Pop. 2,049. -Also a township of Lawrence co., in the state of Ohio. Pop. 1,540.—Also a township of Miami co., in the same state. Pop. 1,381.—Also a village of Wood co., in the state of Virginia, 325 m. NW of Richmond, on the W bank of Little Kanawha river, 25 m. from its mouth.—Also a village of Harrison co., in the state of Indiana, 138 m. S of Indianapolis, 4 m. N of the Ohio. Pop. 100.—Also a village of Jackson co., in the state of Arkansas, 118 m. NE of Little Rock, on the E side of White river, a little below the entrance of Big Black river.
ELIZABETH BAY, an extensive indentation of the W coast of the island of Albemarle, in the Galapagos group, in S lat. 39°, W long. 91° 10'.

ELIZABETH (CAPE), the N extremity of the island of Sagalin, in the sea of Okhotsk, to the NE of Cape Maria, in N lat. 54° 26', E long. 142° 47'.— Also a bold and rocky headland of Russian America. at the S extremity of the Tchukatchis peninsula, and on the E side of Cook's inlet, in N lat. 59° 8', W

ELIZABETH CITY, a county of the state of Virginia, U. S., comprising an area of 64 sq. m., bounded on the E by Chesapeake bay, and on the S generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 3,706. Its capital is Hampton.—Also a village of Pasquotank co., in the state of North Carolina, 40 m. SW of Norfolk, in the state of Virginia, on Pasquotank river, 20 m. from its mouth. Pop. 2.000.

ELIZABETH ISLAND, an island in the South Pacific, to the SE of the Low archipelago, in S lat. 24° 31', W long. 128° 18', about 5 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth. It is of coral formation, and is covered with shrubby vegetation.—Also an island in the Low archipelago, in S lat 16°, W long. 146°.

ELIZABETH ISLAND, or SANTA ISABEL, an island in the strait of Magalhaen, in S lat. 52° 50', W long. 70° 50', stretching across the entrance of Pecket harbour. Its sides are steep and lofty, and it possesses but little vegetation.

ELIZABETH ISLANDS, a group of small islands, 16 in number, extending in a NE direction along the coast of the state of Massachusetts, between Buzzard's bay and Vineyard-sound. The principal are Nashawn, Pasqui, Nashaweena, Piniquese, and Cuttyhunk. Two of them only are inhabited.
ELIZABETH (MOUNT), a summit of SE. Australia, to the W of Wellington co., and 10 m. from the l. bank of the Macquarie.
ELIZABETH (POINT), a headland of New South Wales, in Gloucester co., in S lat. 32° 25'.

ELIZABETH (PORT), an important and rising seaport in the Uitenhage district of the E prov. of Cape colony, in S. Africa, on the W side of Algoa bay, at the mouth of the Baaken's river, 90 m. SW of Graham's Town, 18 m. SSE of Uitenhage, and 3 m. NW of Cape Recife, in S lat. 33° 57'. The anchorage off the port is the best sheltered spot on the coast for 600 m. from Cape-town. The bay is indeed open to all winds from S to E; but with common prudence, and good tackle, large ships may ride out the heaviest gales in this bay, and will seldom find their communication with the shore interrupted. The tide rises here about 6 ft. The bay abounds in fish, and fresh water can be had in abundance. All these advantages led to the foundation of a settlement here in April 1820, by Sir Rufane Donkin. The original pop. of Fort Frederick, a small military fort founded here in 1799, was about 35 souls, but it now exceeds 5,000. A splendid pile of buildings for the use of the commissariat, and a place d'armes, have been erected here at a cost of £14,000; there are also a courthouse, a gaol, an exchange, and churches for various denominations. Nearly all the import and export trade of the districts of Uitenhage, Albany, and part of George, on the coast, and of Beaufort, Somerset, and Graff-Reynet, to the N, centres at Port E. Its imports in 1846 amounted to £201,485; in 1848 to £326,293; in 1849 to £253,685. Its exports in 1846 amounted to £223,031; in 1849 to £93,794; and consisted of wool, aloes, whale-bone, whale-oil, gum, hides, horns, ivory, goat skins, tallow, ostrich-feathers, and bees'-wax. The staple exportable production of the district is wool; and the quantity of it exported from Port E. to England in 1849 was 3,457,734 lbs., and coastwise 196,250 lbs., making a total of 3,653,984 lbs., and an increase of 1,377,766 lbs. on the export for 1848.-A steamer plies between Table-bay and Port E. twice in the month. The following is a statement of the number and tonnage of vessels inwards and outwards at Port E., for the year ended Jan. 5, 1850: ELJ Inwards. Outwards. Tons. Ships. Tons. Ships. Direct, Total, From its proximity to the sea, the temp. of Port E. during summer is nearly 10° lower than in the interior; the soil enjoys a greater share of moisture, dew falls more abundantly, and the climate has maintained a high character for salubrity.

ELIZABETH (PORT), a village of Elizabeth township, Essex co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 47 m. NE of Trenton, and 12 m. SW of New York, on Staten sound, and at the terminus of the Elizabeth-town and Somerville railroad. It has daily communication by steam with New York.

ELIZABETH (Saint), a parish of Jamaica, forming the SE portion of the co. of Cornwall. It is intersected by several ranges of hills, and is watered by Black river, which falls into a large bay of the same name. This p. is one of the most considerable divisions of the island. Its capital is Lacovia.

ELIZABETH RIVER, a river of South-Eastern Australia, which flows into the Macquarie, 7 m. SSW of the confluence of Erskine river.—Also a river of the state of Virginia, U.S., formed by the junction of two streams which take their rise in Norfolk and Princess Anne counties, and unite at Norfolk, thence expand into a considerable estuary, and, after a total course of 25 m., fall into James river near its entrance into Chesapeake bay. This river forms an excellent and commodious harbour. Its W branch unites at Deep creek with Dismal Swamp canal, and forms, with Pasquotank river, a communication between Chesapeake bay and Albemarle sound.
ELIZABETHSTADT. See EBESFALVA.

ELIZABETH-TOWN, a township of Upper Canada, in the Johnson district, bounded on the S by the St. Lawrence. It contains 4 small lakes, and is watered by Petite-Nation and Rideau rivers. Pop. 6,437.—Also a town of Essex co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 44 m. NE of Trenton, on Elizabeth creek, 2½ m. from its entrance into Staten sound, and intersected by the New Jersey and Elizabeth-town and Somerville railroads. Pop. in 1840, 2,500.—Also a village of Elizabeth township, Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 18 m. E of Harrisburg, on the Forks of Coney river. Pop. 200. -Also a village of Whitewater township, Hamilton co., in the state of Ohio, 132 m. SW of Columbus, and intersected by the Whitewater canal. Pop. 200. -Also a village of Marshall co., in the state of Virginia, on Grave creek, at its entrance into the Ohio, 12 m. below Wheeling. In the environs are numerous ancient tumuli.—Also a village of Hardin co., in the state of Kentucky, 79 m. SW of Frankfort, on the N side of Valley creek. Pop. 979.—Also a village of Bladen co., in the state of North Carolina, 99 m. S of Raleigh, on the W side of Cape Fear. Also a village of Carter co., in the state of Tennessee, 301 m. E of Nashville, on the Watanga river, a branch of the Holston river. In the vicinity are extensive manufactories of iron.—Also a village of Hardin co., in the state of Illinois, 219 m. SSE of Springfield. Pop. 1,050.
